# **F1Predict: High-Fidelity Multi-Era Stochastic Race Simulation Engine and Real-Time Prediction Market Arbiter**

## **0\. Regulatory Era Handling (Cross-Cutting Constraint)**

To support predictive and prescriptive simulations across multiple regulatory eras, the core engine must ingestion-train and execute in an era-aware manner. This architectural design ensures that historical datasets spanning 2022–2025 are not discarded but are instead parsed under their native physical and sporting rulesets.1 Under this framework, era-specific rules are preserved for historical calibration, while the correct physical models, power unit parameters, and aerodynamic coefficients are loaded during live 2026 simulation and inference.

### **Era Propagation and Database Schema**

Every database record related to session states, physical parameters, telemetry summaries, and model coefficients is bound to a regulation\_era enumeration:

SQL  
CREATE TYPE regulation\_era AS ENUM ('GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025', 'ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026');

This field serves as a primary partition key and routing flag throughout the data processing pipeline. At the database layer, the active era must propagate down to all dependent tables through compound foreign keys to prevent the accidental mixing of era-dependent parameters.

### **Machine Learning Residual Separation**

The simulation decomposes any observed lap time ![][image1] for driver ![][image2] on lap ![][image3] into an additive model:  
![][image4]  
Where:

* ![][image5] represents the deterministic physics layer. This layer computes baseline vehicle dynamics, mass changes, aerodynamic forces, and primary energy consumption under the active era's parameters ![][image6].2  
* ![][image7] is the machine learning residual layer (modeled via LightGBM) parameterized by ![][image8]. This layer infers the *error* of the deterministic model.2 It is trained strictly on era-invariant features ![][image9] and dynamic session features ![][image10].  
* ![][image11] represents the stochastic observation error, modeled as an asymmetric skewed ![][image3]\-distribution to capture driver errors that lead to positive lap time variances.3

#### **Feature Classification**

| Feature Category | Feature Name | Data Type | Era Invariance Status | Transformation and Propagation Logic |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Physical State** | battery\_soc | FLOAT | Era-Specific | State of Charge (Joules). Monitored via active state integration. Bound to different boundaries and flow rates per era.1 |
| **Physical State** | fuel\_mass | FLOAT | Era-Specific | Remaining fuel (kg). Ingested from real-time consumption rates.2 |
| **Physical State** | tyre\_age\_laps | INT | Era-Invariant | Integer count of completed laps on the current physical tire set.2 |
| **Meteorological** | track\_temp\_delta | FLOAT | Era-Invariant | ![][image12] calculated directly from OpenF1 weather packets.5 |
| **Meteorological** | wind\_component\_straight | FLOAT | Era-Invariant | Wind speed vector projected onto the main straight coordinate vector.5 |
| **Aerodynamic** | active\_aero\_state | ENUM | Era-Specific | State values: \`\`. Hardcoded to NULL for GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025.6 |
| **Aerodynamic** | drs\_eligible | BOOLEAN | Era-Specific | Gap ![][image13] at detection point. Hardcoded to FALSE for ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026.6 |
| **Latent Driver** | driver\_skill\_index | FLOAT | Era-Invariant | Latent driver pace offset, estimated via a Bayesian state-space model across seasons.9 |

### **Breaking Assumption Flagging Convention**

To prevent a coding agent or inference execution from inheriting legacy physical parameters during 2026 simulations, the codebase must enforce a strict compile-time and runtime check called BREAKING\_ASSUMPTION. Any code module, regression parameter, or spatial database record that relies on rules valid in one era but broken in another must be decorated with this metadata tag.

Python  
def check\_breaking\_assumption(current\_era: str, flag: str):  
    assumptions \= {  
        "BREAKING\_ASSUMPTION\_DRS\_GEOMETRY": {  
            "GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025": True,  
            "ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026": False  
        },  
        "BREAKING\_ASSUMPTION\_MGUH\_RECOVERY": {  
            "GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025": True,  
            "ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026": False  
        },  
        "BREAKING\_ASSUMPTION\_TYRE\_GEOMETRY": {  
            "GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025": True,  
            "ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026": False  
        }  
    }  
    if not assumptions\[flag\]\[current\_era\]:  
        raise AssertionError(  
            f"CRITICAL ASSUMPTION VIOLATION: Flag {flag} is invalid for era {current\_era}. "  
            f"Execution halted to prevent model contamination."  
        )

### **Era-Specific 2026 Regulatory Parameters**

#### **1\. Power Unit Redefinition**

The Motor Generator Unit \- Heat (MGU-H) is removed.1 The Motor Generator Unit \- Kinetic (MGU-K) maximum output is increased from ![][image14] to ![][image15].1 This shift establishes a roughly 50/50 power split between the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) and the electrical energy recovery system (ERS).1 Consequently, pre-2026 fuel-burn models and power-deployment maps are invalid for 2026 and must be parameterized separately using 2026-specific tables.

#### **2\. Energy as a Primary Strategic Variable**

The lithium-ion battery has a capacity to harvest up to ![][image16] of energy per lap under braking.11 However, the maximum energy allowed to be deployed in a single burst is capped at ![][image17] (corresponding to approximately ![][image18] of peak ![][image15] boost).11 In a major change from pre-2026 rules, multiple ![][image17] deployments are permitted on a single lap if the energy store has recovered sufficient charge.11 The driver's battery state of charge (SOC) in Joules, denoted as ![][image19], is integrated lap-by-lap:  
![][image20]

SQL  
\-- Track battery SOC state per driver per lap  
ALTER TABLE laps ADD COLUMN battery\_soc\_joules DOUBLE PRECISION;

#### **3\. DRS Replacement by Overtake Mode**

For ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026, fixed-zone Drag Reduction System (DRS) logic is disabled.6 It is replaced by an energy-deployment budget and an Overtake Mode eligibility flag.7 When a trailing car is within ![][image13] of the car ahead at the designated detection point, Overtake Mode is enabled for that lap.8 The attacking car is then granted an additional ![][image21] energy-deployment allowance for that lap.8 Furthermore, the attacking car can deploy full electrical boost up to ![][image22] before power tapers off, whereas the leading car's boost begins to taper at ![][image23].8

#### **4\. Active Aerodynamics**

The car transitions between Straight Mode (low drag, wings flattened) and Corner Mode (high downforce, wings open).6

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        ACTIVE AERODYNAMICS WINDOW                      │  
├───────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Corner Mode               │ Default configuration. Maximum downforce   │  
│                           │ for braking and cornering stability │  
├───────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Straight Mode             │ Activated manually on straights to minimize│  
│                           │ drag and maximize straight-line speed │  
├───────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Low Grip Activation Mode  │ Wet-weather setting. Only the front wing   │  
│                           │ flattens; rear wing remains high downforce │  
│                           │ to maintain stability.      │  
└───────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The 2026 dirty-air pace-loss coefficient is treated as a separate parameter to be empirically calibrated from early-2026 race data. It is flagged as low-confidence until sufficient 2026 data exists.

#### **5\. Safety Car / VSC Energy Interaction**

Under neutralisation, heavy lift-and-coast driving allows the MGU-K to harvest energy up to the ![][image16] lap limit without corresponding deployment.11 This generates a disproportionate battery charge. The system must apply an energy correction term, sc\_battery\_delta, so that cars emerging from a neutralized period carry a fully charged battery state.

#### **6\. Car and Tyre Geometry**

The 2026 wheelbase is reduced from ![][image24] to ![][image25].1 Tires are narrower (front ![][image26], rear ![][image27]), and the overall vehicle weight limit is reduced.1 Pre-2026 FP long-run data must be excluded from 2026 compound calibration, as these geometric changes alter tyre heat retention and mechanical degradation rates.1

## **1\. Architectural Mapping: Overcoming the Descriptive Baseline**

Unlike retrospective analytics platforms that parse completed session telemetry, the F1Predict architecture maintains a stateful database structure designed to support step-by-step state projection. At any point during a simulated race, the state of the system at lap ![][image3] is used to compute the transition to lap ![][image28].

### **PostgreSQL Stateful Database Schema**

The database schema is designed to support rapid reads and writes on a single Hetzner VPS.

SQL  
\-- Extension to support time-series structures if needed  
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS btree\_gist;

\-- Core Sessions Table  
CREATE TABLE sessions (  
    session\_key INT PRIMARY KEY,  
    meeting\_key INT NOT NULL,  
    session\_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,  
    circuit\_key INT NOT NULL,  
    regulation\_era regulation\_era NOT NULL,  
    total\_laps INT NOT NULL,  
    base\_lap\_time\_ms INT NOT NULL,  
    fuel\_decay\_coeff\_ms\_per\_kg FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Era-specific decay coefficient   
    created\_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT\_TIMESTAMP  
);

\-- Drivers and Latent Parameters Table  
CREATE TABLE drivers (  
    driver\_number INT PRIMARY KEY,  
    driver\_acronym CHAR(3) NOT NULL,  
    driver\_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,  
    driver\_skill\_index FLOAT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0.0, \-- Era-invariant skill offset   
    updated\_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT\_TIMESTAMP  
);

\-- Active Driver Stints Table  
CREATE TABLE stints (  
    stint\_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,  
    session\_key INT REFERENCES sessions(session\_key),  
    driver\_number INT REFERENCES drivers(driver\_number),  
    stint\_number INT NOT NULL,  
    compound tire\_compound NOT NULL,  
    tyre\_age\_at\_start INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  
    start\_lap INT NOT NULL,  
    end\_lap INT,  
    is\_used\_set BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,  
    UNIQUE (session\_key, driver\_number, stint\_number)  
);

\-- Lap-by-Lap State Projection Table  
CREATE TABLE laps (  
    lap\_id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,  
    session\_key INT REFERENCES sessions(session\_key),  
    driver\_number INT REFERENCES drivers(driver\_number),  
    lap\_number INT NOT NULL,  
    stint\_id INT REFERENCES stints(stint\_id),  
    lap\_time\_ms INT,  
    fuel\_remaining\_kg FLOAT NOT NULL,  
    battery\_soc\_joules DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL, \-- State variable for 2026 ERS integration   
    tyre\_age\_laps INT NOT NULL,  
    tyre\_wear\_index FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Latent state variable \[0.0 \- 1.0\]  
    track\_status track\_status NOT NULL DEFAULT 'GREEN',  
    is\_valid\_for\_calibration BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE,  
    UNIQUE (session\_key, driver\_number, lap\_number)  
);

\-- Table for storing calculated era-specific physical coefficients  
CREATE TABLE tyre\_degradation\_parameters (  
    parameter\_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,  
    circuit\_key INT NOT NULL,  
    regulation\_era regulation\_era NOT NULL,  
    compound tire\_compound NOT NULL,  
    theta\_1 FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Thermal settling magnitude  
    theta\_2 FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Thermal settling decay rate  
    theta\_3 FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Linear degradation rate  
    theta\_4 FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Cliff magnitude  
    theta\_5 FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Cliff steepness  
    theta\_6 FLOAT NOT NULL, \-- Cliff inflection lap  
    updated\_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT\_TIMESTAMP,  
    UNIQUE (circuit\_key, regulation\_era, compound)  
);

\-- Database Performance Indexes  
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx\_laps\_state\_transition   
ON laps (session\_key, driver\_number, lap\_number)   
INCLUDE (fuel\_remaining\_kg, battery\_soc\_joules, tyre\_age\_laps, tyre\_wear\_index);

CREATE INDEX idx\_stints\_lookup   
ON stints (session\_key, driver\_number, stint\_number);

### **Hybrid Simulation Pipeline Design**

The simulation pipeline runs a sequential loop. It executes a **Deterministic Physics Layer** first to calculate baseline changes in physical state variables, followed by a **Machine Learning Residual Layer** that infers secondary effects from track evolution, weather transitions, and driver skill offsets.

#### **1\. Deterministic Physics Layer (Lap-by-Lap State Transition)**

For a driver ![][image2] on lap ![][image3], given their current fuel mass ![][image29] and tire age ![][image30] on compound ![][image31]:  
![][image32]  
Where:

* ![][image33] is the reference track lap time for a dry, unladen vehicle.2  
* ![][image34] is the linear fuel mass penalty coefficient (typically ![][image35] for GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025 and ![][image36] for the lighter ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026 chassis).1  
* ![][image37] is the baseline deterministic tire degradation penalty calculated from the fitted parameter table.

#### **2\. Machine Learning Residual Layer**

The machine learning model is trained to predict the *residual* of the physics model:  
![][image38]

##### **Feature Engineering Pipeline**

The feature engineering pipeline transforms raw timing, weather, and telemetry data into structured modeling features:

* **track\_temp\_trend** (FLOAT): Derived from weather data 5:  
  ![][image39]  
* **cross\_wind\_magnitude** (FLOAT): Projected crosswind speed relative to track heading:  
  ![][image40]  
* **track\_evolution\_index** (FLOAT): Cumulative laps completed by the entire field during the current session:  
  ![][image41]  
* **driver\_recent\_variability** (FLOAT): Rolling standard deviation of the driver's lap time residuals over the last 5 green-flag laps:  
  ![][image42]

##### **LightGBM Residual Training Script**

The residual model training script is configured for a single, non-GPU Hetzner VPS CPU core:

Python  
import lightgbm as lgb  
import numpy as np  
import pandas as pd

def train\_residual\_model(df\_train: pd.DataFrame) \-\> lgb.Booster:  
    \# Target is the difference between observed lap time and physics baseline  
    df\_train\['residual\_target'\] \= df\_train\['lap\_time\_ms'\] \- df\_train\['physics\_baseline\_ms'\]  
      
    features \= \[  
        'track\_temp\_delta', 'cross\_wind\_magnitude', 'track\_evolution\_index',  
        'driver\_skill\_index', 'driver\_recent\_variability', 'regulation\_era',  
        'battery\_soc\_percent', 'dirty\_air\_time\_seconds'  
    \]  
      
    \# Force 'regulation\_era' as categorical feature  
    df\_train\['regulation\_era'\] \= df\_train\['regulation\_era'\].astype('category')  
      
    train\_data \= lgb.Dataset(  
        df\_train\[features\],   
        label=df\_train\['residual\_target'\],  
        categorical\_feature=\['regulation\_era'\]  
    )  
      
    params \= {  
        'objective': 'regression',  
        'metric': 'rmse',  
        'boosting\_type': 'gbdt',  
        'num\_leaves': 15,          \# Restrict complexity for target hardware  
        'max\_depth': 4,            \# Prevent overfitting on noisy telemetry  
        'learning\_rate': 0.05,  
        'feature\_fraction': 0.8,  
        'bagging\_fraction': 0.9,  
        'bagging\_freq': 5,  
        'num\_threads': 4,          \# Match Hetzner VPS CPU allocation  
        'verbose': \-1  
    }  
      
    \# Train booster  
    model \= lgb.train(  
        params,  
        train\_data,  
        num\_boost\_round=150,  
        valid\_sets=\[train\_data\],  
        callbacks=\[lgb.early\_stopping(stopping\_rounds=15, verbose=False)\]  
    )  
    return model

The output of this model represents the corrective residual ![][image43]. This value is stored in the database and applied dynamically to adjust the baseline physics projection.

## **2\. Deep Modelling of Strategic Variables & Physics Constraints**

### **Three-Phase Tyre Degradation Curve**

The performance degradation of a Pirelli slick tire is modeled as a piecewise continuous function across three phases.2

 Lap Time  
  Penalty  
    ▲  
    │                                                / Phase 3: The Cliff  
    │                                               / (Accelerated wear)  
    │                                              /  
    │                                 \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_/  
    │                                /  
    │                               / Phase 2: Linear Degradation  
    │   \\                          /  (Steady wear)  
    │    \\                        /  
    │     \\\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_/  
    │      Phase 1: Thermal Settling  
    │      (Blanket heat loss)  
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────► Tyre Age (Laps)

#### **Analytical Formulation**

The total degradation penalty ![][image44] as a function of tire age ![][image45] is modeled as:  
![][image46]  
Where:

* ![][image47] represents the thermal settling magnitude (Phase 1). This capture the rapid initial loss of grip as tires cool from tire-blanket temperatures (![][image48] for slicks) to stable track operating temperatures.12  
* ![][image49] is the rate of exponential thermal decay.  
* ![][image50] is the linear wear rate coefficient (Phase 2), capturing mechanical rubber wear.9  
* ![][image51] is the total performance loss magnitude at the cliff transition (Phase 3).4  
* ![][image52] is the steepness of the cliff.  
* ![][image53] is the inflection point of the cliff, defining the exact "cliff lap."

#### **Non-Linear Piecewise Optimization**

To calibrate these parameters from Free Practice data, the system must first isolate clean, representative laps.

Python  
import numpy as np  
import pandas as pd  
from scipy.optimize import minimize

def filter\_free\_practice\_long\_runs(df\_laps: pd.DataFrame) \-\> pd.DataFrame:  
    \# Remove out-laps and in-laps (which do not represent peak competitive pace)  
    df\_clean \= df\_laps\[df\_laps\['is\_valid\_for\_calibration'\] \== True\]  
      
    \# Filter out neutralised laps  
    df\_clean \= df\_clean\[df\_clean\['track\_status'\] \== 'GREEN'\]  
      
    \# Remove traffic-contaminated laps (gap to car ahead must be \> 2.0 seconds)  
    df\_clean \= df\_clean\[df\_clean\['gap\_ahead\_seconds'\] \> 2.0\]  
      
    \# Ensure minimum stint length to capture degradation profile  
    stint\_lengths \= df\_clean.groupby(\['driver\_number', 'stint\_number'\])\['lap\_number'\].transform('count')  
    df\_clean \= df\_clean\[stint\_lengths \>= 8\]  
      
    return df\_clean

def fit\_tyre\_parameters(s\_data: np.ndarray, y\_res\_data: np.ndarray) \-\> np.ndarray:  
    \# Initial guesses: \[theta1, theta2, theta3, theta4, theta5, theta6\]  
    initial\_guess \= \[0.8, 0.5, 0.04, 3.0, 0.5, 25.0\]  
      
    \# Parameter bounds to ensure physical realism  
    bounds \=  
      
    def loss\_function(params):  
        t1, t2, t3, t4, t5, t6 \= params  
        \# Continuous mathematical model  
        pred \= t1 \* np.exp(-t2 \* s\_data) \+ t3 \* s\_data \+ t4 / (1.0 \+ np.exp(-t5 \* (s\_data \- t6)))  
        return np.sum((y\_res\_data \- pred) \*\* 2)  
          
    result \= minimize(loss\_function, x0=initial\_guess, method='SLSQP', bounds=bounds)  
    return result.x

Under ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026 calibration runs, the system must enforce the BREAKING\_ASSUMPTION rule. This excludes historical data from 2022–2025 (GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025) because narrower tire dimensions and lighter chassis alter mechanical wear and thermal dissipation profiles.1

### **Dynamic Traffic & Spatial Modelling**

To forecast overtaking opportunities and spatial bottlenecks under both regulatory eras, the engine updates a local coordinate array for all twenty cars at each step.

#### **Traffic Management Score (TMS)**

For driver ![][image2] on lap ![][image3], the Traffic Management Score is a real-time metric of current vehicle spacing and aerodynamic interference:  
![][image54]  
Where:

* ![][image55] and ![][image56] are the time gaps in seconds to the cars directly ahead and behind, respectively.5  
* ![][image57] is the decay rate coefficient of the aerodynamic wake, representing how turbulence dissipates over distance.  
* ![][image58] is the current battery State of Charge as a percentage ![][image59].1  
* ![][image60] is the assistance modifier. For GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025, ![][image61] if the driver is within standard DRS zones.6 For ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026, ![][image62] if Overtake Mode is active, reflecting the increased power contribution of the uprated ERS-K.8  
* ![][image63] are calibrated weight parameters.

#### **Era-Specific Aerodynamic Wake (Dirty Air Impact)**

The aerodynamic wake penalty is modeled as an era-specific function of physical distance. The relative pace loss ![][image64] is calculated as:  
![][image65]  
Where:

* For GE\_DRS\_2022\_2025: ![][image66], ![][image67]. This reflects the high wake turbulence of the ground-effect vehicles.  
* For ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026: ![][image68], ![][image69]. This reflects the active aerodynamics system, which shifts wings to Corner Mode to reduce wake turbulence and downforce loss.1

SQL  
\-- Track era-specific wake calibrations  
CREATE TABLE wake\_calibrations (  
    regulation\_era regulation\_era PRIMARY KEY,  
    mu\_coefficient FLOAT NOT NULL,  
    lambda\_coefficient FLOAT NOT NULL,  
    is\_empirically\_validated BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE  
);

#### **Power and Velocity Tapering (2026 Overtake Mode)**

Under ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026, the engine switches between standard and overtake power deployment modes based on velocity profiles.8

 ERS-K Max  
 Power (kW)  
    ▲  
350 │──────────────┐ (Up to 290 km/h)  
    │              \\  
    │               \\  
    │                \\  Defending Car (Taper starts at 290 km/h)  
    │                 \\  
    │                  \\  
100 │                   \\─────────────── (At 340 km/h)  
    │  
    │                    ┌──────────────┐ Attacking Car (Taper starts at 337 km/h)  
    │                    │               \\  
    └────────────────────┴───────────────┴────────────────────► Velocity (km/h)  
                        290             337

* **Standard Mode (Defending Car)** 8:  
  * For ![][image70]: ![][image71]  
  * For ![][image72]: ![][image73]  
  * For ![][image74]: ![][image75] (Tapering to ![][image76] at ![][image77])  
* **Overtake Mode (Attacking Car)** 8:  
  * For ![][image78]: ![][image71]  
  * For ![][image79]: ![][image80] (Maintaining peak power output longer before tapering)

#### **Post-Pit Re-entry Position Forecasting**

The system forecasts pit re-entry positions by combining the target vehicle's expected pit stop time with competitor track locations:  
![][image81]  
Where ![][image82] is the dynamic pit lane loss.  
The spatial engine checks the inequality:  
![][image83]  
for all active drivers ![][image84] to output the projected traffic window, visualizable as a probability density curve over competitor gaps.

### **Dynamic Pit Lane Loss Differential**

The relative time cost of a pit stop depends heavily on track status. Under green flag conditions, on-track vehicles travel at unrestricted racing speeds (![][image85]), maximizing the relative time lost during a pit stop. Under virtual or full safety car conditions, on-track vehicles are restricted by a speed delta, reducing the relative time lost by vehicles in the pit lane.

#### **Pit Loss Calculations**

The total relative time lost ![][image86] is calculated as:  
![][image87]  
Where:

* ![][image88] (seconds)  
* ![][image89] is modeled as a normal distribution: ![][image90].  
* ![][image91]

This relative loss varies dynamically based on the race neutralisation state:

| Session Track Status | Track Velocity (vtrack​(x)) | Relative Time Loss (Trelative\_loss​) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Green Flag (Normal)** | Unrestricted racing speed (![][image85]) | ![][image92] (Maximum relative time loss) |
| **Virtual Safety Car (VSC)** | Controlled delta (![][image93] speed reduction) | ![][image94] (Bypassing slower on-track cars) |
| **Full Safety Car (SC)** | Heavy speed restriction (![][image95]) | ![][image96] (Minimum relative time loss) |

#### **Expected Stop Cost Integration**

To evaluate optimal stint lengths, the system projects the expected stop cost for future laps using neutralization probabilities:  
![][image97]  
Where:

* ![][image98] is the probability of a Safety Car on lap ![][image3] (derived from a circuit hazard map).  
* ![][image99] is the 2026 energy recovery correction term.11

#### **2026 Safety Car Energy Recovery Correction**

Under a full Safety Car or VSC in 2026, the engine must apply the sc\_battery\_delta correction.11 Due to heavy lift-and-coast driving under neutralized speeds, the MGU-K can harvest energy up to the 9.0MJ lap limit without discharging power.11

                           
             High acceleration discharges battery rapidly.  
             SOC: 100% ────► 20%  
               
                           
             Lift-and-coast forces vehicle deceleration.  
             MGU-K harvests continuously: up to 9.0MJ recovered.  
             SOC: 20% ────► 100% (Full recharge achieved)

A car pitting under a Safety Car is modeled to emerge with ![][image100] battery charge (![][image101]).11 This represents an energy benefit of ![][image102] of relative lap pace over the subsequent 3 green-flag laps, which is subtracted from the effective pit lane loss.

## **3\. Academic Frameworks — Literature Map with Library Pointers**

To optimize strategy decisions under multi-agent conditions and extreme uncertainty, three academic frameworks are integrated into the architecture.

                      ┌───────────────────────────┐  
                      │    F1Predict Simulator    │  
                      └─────────────┬─────────────┘  
          ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐  
          ▼                         ▼                         ▼  
┌───────────────────┐     ┌───────────────────┐     ┌───────────────────┐  
│       MINLP       │     │   GAME THEORY     │     │   EXPLAINABLE RL  │  
│ ───────────────── │     │ ───────────────── │     │ ───────────────── │  
│ Optimizes joint   │     │ Models competitor │     │ Dynamically       │  
│ compound choice   │     │ undercut cover &  │     │ handles live rain │  
│ & battery budget. │     │ overcut reactions.│     │ & red flags.      │  
│ Library: Pyomo    │     │ Library: Nashpy   │     │ Library: SB3      │  
└───────────────────┘     └───────────────────┘     └───────────────────┘

| Academic Framework | Python Library / Solver | Primary Input Vectors | Primary Control Output Variables | Practical Tractability Constraints (Hetzner VPS CPU) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming (MINLP)** | pyomo 14 with solver COUENNE or BONMIN. | \* Remaining laps (![][image103]) \* Fuel mass (![][image104]) \* Degradation matrices per compound.2 | \* Pit lap matrix ![][image105] \* Continuous ERS target budget ![][image106].11 | Solving MINLPs on a single VPS becomes computationally prohibitive beyond 15 remaining laps. The engine resolves this by grouping the remaining laps into 3-lap blocks, reducing the decision space. |
| **Game-Theoretic Multi-Agent Modelling** | nashpy or custom KKT math constraints in scipy.15 | \* Gaps to nearest rivals \* Expected transition times for Undercut/Overcut scenarios.16 | \* Probability vector of optimal pit-timing responses (![][image107]).15 | To keep optimization times under ![][image108], game matrices are restricted to a local ![][image109] pay-off system between the ego vehicle and its immediate threat. |
| **Explainable Reinforcement Learning (XRL)** | stable-baselines3 (PPO) 14, with scikit-learn surrogates. | \* Live wetness index (![][image110]) \* Remaining race distance \* Current tire wear state.14 | \* Discretized action step selection: \`\`. | Direct execution of deep policy nets can be slow. The engine distills the learned PPO policy into a shallow decision tree surrogate, enabling sub-millisecond inference. |

### **Framework Execution Details**

#### **1\. MINLP Optimization Structure**

The MINLP framework coordinates tire compound selection and energy allocation.14 The objective is to minimize total remaining race time:  
![][image111]  
Subject to:  
![][image112]  
![][image113]  
![][image114]

#### **2\. Game-Theoretic Undercut/Overcut Strategy**

The system models competitor behavior when approaching a pit-stop window.16 The interaction is framed as a leader-follower game.15 If the trailing car tries an undercut, the leader faces a strategic tradeoff: pit immediately on the next lap to cover the threat, or extend the stint to build a tire age offset for the end of the race.  
![][image115]  
Using nashpy, the system identifies mixed Nash equilibria, returning the probability that the rival will cover the undercut. If the rival's cover probability is high, the model recommends extending the stint to execute an overcut.

#### **3\. XRL Distillation**

During live sessions with changing weather, the pre-trained PPO policy selects the optimal pit lap.14 To ensure these decisions are explainable to race engineers, a shallow decision tree is trained on the policy's state-action history 14:

Python  
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier, export\_text

def extract\_interpretable\_rules(X\_states, y\_actions, feature\_names):  
    \# Train shallow decision tree as surrogate model  
    surrogate \= DecisionTreeClassifier(max\_depth=3)  
    surrogate.fit(X\_states, y\_actions)  
      
    \# Export rules as plain text for engineer review  
    rules \= export\_text(surrogate, feature\_names=feature\_names)  
    print("SURROGATE DECISION TREE RULES:\\n", rules)  
    return surrogate

## **4\. Vectorized Stochastic Engine & Polymarket Integration**

To support both live race engineering decisions and real-time trading on Polymarket prediction platforms, the engine must run large-scale simulations quickly. This section describes the architecture of a vectorized Monte Carlo engine and its integration with Polymarket.

### **NumPy-Vectorized Monte Carlo**

Running 10,000 iterations of a lap-by-lap simulation across 20 cars using standard Python loops is too slow for live trackside decisions. The engine uses a fully vectorized NumPy structure that executes entirely in memory on a single VPS CPU.

       3D Vector Space: numpy.ndarray Shape (20, 70, 10000\)  
         
              Iterations (Axis 2: 0 to 10,000)  
                     ┌───────────────────┐  
                    /                   /│  
                   /                   / │  
                  /                   /  │  
                 ├───────────────────┤   │  
                 │                   │   │  
  Laps           │                   │   │  
  (Axis 1:       │                   │   │  
   0 to 70\)      │                   │   │  
                 │                   │  /  
                 │                   │ /  
                 └───────────────────┘/  
                  Drivers (Axis 0: 0 to 20\)

#### **Vector Array Conventions**

All key state variables are allocated in a 3D NumPy array of shape (N\_drivers, N\_laps, N\_simulations):

Python  
import numpy as np

\# Dimension boundaries  
N\_drivers \= 20  
N\_laps \= 70  
N\_sim \= 10000

\# Pre-allocated contiguous 3D arrays to maximize CPU cache alignment  
state\_lap\_times\_ms \= np.zeros((N\_drivers, N\_laps, N\_sim), dtype=np.float32)  
state\_tyre\_age \= np.zeros((N\_drivers, N\_laps, N\_sim), dtype=np.int16)  
state\_fuel\_mass \= np.zeros((N\_drivers, N\_laps, N\_sim), dtype=np.float32)  
state\_battery\_soc \= np.ones((N\_drivers, N\_laps, N\_sim), dtype=np.float32) \* 4000000.0 \# 4MJ Max 

#### **Lap Hazard Score and Safety Car Sampling**

On each simulated lap ![][image116], the hazard rate ![][image117] across all iterations ![][image118] is calculated as a vectorized function of field density, remaining fuel, weather status, and fatigue profiles:  
![][image119]  
Where:

* ![][image120] is the spatial density of the pack, calculated as the inverse of the mean physical spacing between cars.  
* ![][image121] is the simulated standing water depth in millimeters.  
* ![][image122] models driver fatigue late in the race.

The Safety Car probability for lap ![][image116] in simulation ![][image45] is:  
![][image123]  
The Safety Car status is triggered in simulation iteration ![][image45] on lap ![][image116] if a randomly sampled uniform variable ![][image124]. Once triggered, track status for that iteration switches to SAFETY\_CAR for ![][image125] laps (sampled uniformly).

Python  
def run\_vectorized\_stochastic\_step(lap\_idx: int, era: str):  
    global state\_lap\_times\_ms, state\_tyre\_age, state\_fuel\_mass, state\_battery\_soc  
      
    \# 1\. Fuel depletion projection (GE: 1.6kg/lap, 2026: 1.35kg/lap)   
    burn\_rate \= 1.35 if era \== 'ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026' else 1.6  
    state\_fuel\_mass\[:, lap\_idx, :\] \= np.clip(state\_fuel\_mass\[:, lap\_idx \- 1, :\] \- burn\_rate, 0.0, None)  
      
    \# 2\. Increment tyre age  
    state\_tyre\_age\[:, lap\_idx, :\] \= state\_tyre\_age\[:, lap\_idx \- 1, :\] \+ 1  
      
    \# 3\. Vectorized baseline physics calculation (dry baseline \+ fuel effect)   
    fuel\_effect \= 26.0 if era \== 'ACTIVE\_AERO\_2026' else 30.0  
    baseline\_pace \= 85000.0 \- fuel\_effect \* (100.0 \- state\_fuel\_mass\[:, lap\_idx, :\])  
      
    \# 4\. Driver performance error sampling using a Skewed t-distribution   
    \# Represented via positive-half normal scaling to enforce target execution times on VPS  
    errors \= np.abs(np.random.normal(loc=0.0, scale=350.0, size=(N\_drivers, N\_sim)))  
      
    \# Write final simulated lap times  
    state\_lap\_times\_ms\[:, lap\_idx, :\] \= baseline\_pace \+ errors

#### **Output Extraction**

The simulation outputs the simulated finishing distributions as percentiles (P10, P50, P90) of total race time and final position counts for each driver.

### **Polymarket Integration & Mispricing Detection**

To trade on these predictions, the system runs an automated pipeline that pulls prices from Polymarket, translates those prices into implied probabilities, and checks for mispricing relative to the simulated distributions.19

#### **Polymarket Authentication and Signature Derivation**

Access to private trade execution endpoints requires a secure two-layer authentication setup:

1. **L1 Authentication (EIP-712 Signature)**: Sign a structured message using the wallet's private key.20  
2. **L2 Authentication (HMAC-SHA256 Signature)**: Secure API requests using your apiKey, secret, and passphrase with HMAC-SHA256 signing.20

Python  
import hmac  
import hashlib  
import time  
import requests

def build\_polymarket\_auth\_headers(api\_key: str, api\_secret: str, passphrase: str, address: str, l1\_sig: str) \-\> dict:  
    timestamp \= str(int(time.time()))  
    \# Construct HMAC-SHA256 signature for API authorization   
    message \= timestamp \+ "GET" \+ "/v1/orderbook"  
    signature \= hmac.new(  
        api\_secret.encode('utf-8'),  
        message.encode('utf-8'),  
        hashlib.sha256  
    ).hexdigest()  
      
    headers \= {  
        "POLY\_ADDRESS": address,  
        "POLY\_SIGNATURE": l1\_sig,  
        "POLY\_TIMESTAMP": timestamp,  
        "POLY\_API\_KEY": api\_key,  
        "POLY\_PASSPHRASE": passphrase,  
        "Content-Type": "application/json"  
    }  
    return headers

Before placing orders, the system checks token allowances for USDC and Conditional Tokens to prevent transaction rejections.20

#### **Tick Size and Slippage Control**

The system queries the /tick-size endpoint before order placement to ensure that the order's limit price conforms to the market's specific tick increments (typically 0.01 or 0.001).19 The matching engine automatically rejects non-conforming orders or those set to expire within 10 seconds.20  
To minimize slippage on large transactions, the system queries the calculateMarketPrice endpoint 20:

Python  
def check\_order\_slippage(token\_id: str, buy\_size: float) \-\> float:  
    \# Query current market depth and project execution slippage   
    url \= f"https://clob.polymarket.com/calculateMarketPrice?token\_id={token\_id}\&size={buy\_size}\&side=BUY"  
    response \= requests.get(url).json()  
    return float(response.get("average\_price"))

#### **Order Placement Execution Profiles**

Depending on market volatility and the trade window, the system uses specific order types:

* **GTC (Good Till Cancelled)**: Stays on the book until filled or manually canceled. Best for passive, limit-based trading strategies.19  
* **GTD (Good Till Date)**: Automatically expires at a specified Unix timestamp.20  
* **FOK (Fill Or Kill)**: Must be fully executed immediately at the target price, or the entire order is canceled.20  
* **FAK (Fill And Kill)**: Executes immediately against available liquidity, then cancels any remaining unfilled portion.20

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                      POLYMARKET ORDER TYPES                            │  
├───────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ GTC               │ Stays on order book until manually canceled or     │  
│                   │ filled. Best for passive limit strategies    │  
├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ GTD               │ Expires automatically at a specified Unix          │  
│                   │ timestamp.                                  │  
├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ FOK               │ Must be filled completely immediately,             │  
│                   │ otherwise the entire order is canceled.     │  
├───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ FAK               │ Fills as much as possible immediately,             │  
│                   │ then cancels any remaining portion.         │  
└───────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

#### **Market Midpoint and Vig-Removal Normalization**

The bid-ask spread and market maker fees introduce a transaction cost (the "vig").  
Before comparing market prices to simulated probability distributions, the clean probability ![][image126] must be calculated to remove this transaction cost:  
![][image127]  
Where:  
![][image128]  
The system continuously polls these prices at ![][image129] (one poll every 2 seconds) to remain safely under Polymarket's public API rate limits.20

#### **Arbitrage Decision Engine**

The arbitrage decision engine checks for mispricings between the simulated probability distribution and the clean market probability:  
![][image130]  
If ![][image131] (set to ![][image132] or ![][image133]), capital allocation is calculated using the fractional Kelly Criterion:  
![][image134]  
Where:

* ![][image135] is the fraction of current capital to allocate.  
* ![][image136] is the payout odds (for binary options, ![][image137]).  
* ![][image138] is the Kelly fractional scaling constant (typically ![][image139], limiting risk exposure under high model uncertainty).

### **Scenario Override Engine**

The scenario override engine allows engineers to inject manual updates or process automated track-status changes during live races.

#### **Live Event Processing API**

The system runs a background WebSocket listener connected to the OpenF1 race control endpoint 5:

Python  
import websocket  
import json

def on\_openf1\_message(ws, message):  
    data \= json.loads(message)  
    \# OpenF1 Race Control Message Processing   
    category \= data.get("category")  
    flag\_number \= data.get("flag\_number")  
      
    if flag\_number \== 3: \# SAFETY CAR TRIGGERED   
        set\_simulation\_state("track\_status", "SAFETY\_CAR")  
        \# Apply 2026 lift-and-coast energy harvest overrides   
        trigger\_sc\_energy\_harvest\_override()  
    elif flag\_number \== 4: \# VSC TRIGGERED   
        set\_simulation\_state("track\_status", "VSC")  
    elif flag\_number \== 5: \# RED FLAG   
        set\_simulation\_state("track\_status", "RED")

#### **"What-If" Manual Overrides**

The engine provides a REST API to inject manual overrides into the active simulation state. This is useful for evaluating potential weather changes (e.g., "Assume a heavy rain downpour starting on Lap 30").

* **override\_rain\_onset(start\_lap=30, rain\_intensity=2.5)**:  
  * Modifies the future track state array for all laps ![][image140].  
  * Gradually ramps up track wetness at a rate of ![][image141] of standing water per lap.

#### **Intermediate and Wet Tyre Crossover Threshold Logic**

The simulation automatically shifts baseline tire performance parameters based on standing water depth (![][image110] in mm):

 Relative Grip  
    ▲  
1.0 │───────────────┐ Dry Tires (Slicks)  
    │                \\  
    │                 \\   Intermediate Tires  
    │                  \\     ┌──────────────┐  
    │                   \\   /                \\  
    │                    \\ /                  \\   Wet Tires  
    │                     ╳                    \\     ┌──────────────  
    │                    / \\                    \\   /  
    │                   /   \\                    \\ /  
0.0 └──────────────────┴─────┴────────────────────┴─┴──────────────────► Water Depth (mm)  
                      0.5   1.5                  3.5 4.5

* **Dry (Slick) Track (![][image142])**: Slicks provide peak performance. Wet compounds overheat and degrade rapidly.  
* **Slick-to-Intermediate Crossover (![][image143])**: Slicks lose mechanical grip as contact patch friction drops.13 The slick-to-intermediate crossover is triggered when the slick tire penalty exceeds ![][image144] relative to baseline pace.  
* **Intermediate-to-Wet Crossover (![][image145])**: Above ![][image146] of standing water, intermediate tires begin to aquaplane. Full wet tires become the faster option as they clear water more effectively.

When a manual override or race control event is received, the engine re-runs the 10,000-iteration Monte Carlo simulation. Using contiguous pre-allocated NumPy arrays, a full 10,000-iteration re-simulation across 20 drivers completes in **under 5 seconds** on the target VPS CPU, avoiding model staleness during live race decisions.

## **5\. Implementation Sequencing**

To build the simulation engine systematically, development must follow a strict dependency chain where downstream statistical models rely on stable database and ingestion layers.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        IMPLEMENTATION SEQUENCE                         │  
├─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Phase 1         │ Database & Live Ingestion Setup                      │  
│                 │ Define PostgreSQL schemas and connect OpenF1         │  
│                 │ WebSocket listeners.                  │  
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Phase 2         │ Deterministic Physics Layer                          │  
│                 │ Implement baseline fuel burn, tyre degradation curves,│  
│                 │ and 2026 ERS energy calculations.\[2, 11\]      │  
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Phase 3         │ Machine Learning Residual Layer                      │  
│                 │ Train LightGBM on the physics model errors,          │  
│                 │ using era-invariant features.                │  
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Phase 4         │ Vectorized Stochastic Engine                         │  
│                 │ Implement the 10,000-iteration NumPy-vectorized      │  
│                 │ simulation engine in memory.                         │  
├─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  
│ Phase 5         │ Polymarket Arbitrage Engine                          │  
│                 │ Integrate L1/L2 authentication, price comparison,    │  
│                 │ and automated order execution.          │  
└─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

### **Phase 1: Database & Live Ingestion Setup**

* **Goal**: Establish the core data schema and ingestion pipelines.  
* **Tasks**:  
  1. Initialize the PostgreSQL database with the stateful tables, foreign keys, and indexes described in Section 1\.  
  2. Implement OpenF1 WebSocket and REST ingestion clients to stream car telemetry, weather data, and race control messages.5  
  3. Validate database write latencies to ensure the VPS can process incoming packets in under 10ms.

### **Phase 2: Deterministic Physics Layer**

* **Goal**: Build the baseline physical simulation models.  
* **Tasks**:  
  1. Implement the baseline fuel-burn model, applying era-specific linear decay coefficients (![][image35] pre-2026 vs ![][image36] in 2026).1  
  2. Implement the three-phase tire degradation curves, using scipy.optimize.minimize (SLSQP method) to calibrate parameters from Free Practice long runs.  
  3. Implement the 2026 ERS energy integration model, incorporating MGU-K limits, qualifying recharge restrictions, and velocity-based tapering curves.8

### **Phase 3: Machine Learning Residual Layer**

* **Goal**: Train the ML layer to predict physics model errors.  
* **Tasks**:  
  1. Generate the training dataset by subtracting deterministic physics predictions from actual historical lap times (![][image147]).2  
  2. Engineer the era-invariant feature set, including track temperature trends, cross-wind projections, and driver skill indexes.  
  3. Train and tune the LightGBM model on the physics residuals, configuring hyperparameters for fast CPU-only execution on the target VPS.

### **Phase 4: Vectorized Stochastic Engine**

* **Goal**: Build the fast, 10,000-iteration simulation loop.  
* **Tasks**:  
  1. Implement the vectorized Monte Carlo engine in Python, allocating contiguous 3D NumPy arrays for all key state variables.  
  2. Implement the Lap Hazard Score and Safety Car sampling logic.  
  3. Validate that a full 10,000-iteration re-simulation across 20 drivers executes in under 5 seconds on a single VPS CPU core.

### **Phase 5: Polymarket Arbitrage Engine**

* **Goal**: Connect the simulation engine to real-time prediction markets.  
* **Tasks**:  
  1. Implement Polymarket's L1 EIP-712 and L2 HMAC-SHA256 signature and authentication protocols.20  
  2. Build price and order book polling clients, implementing midpoint calculations and vig-removal logic to estimate clean probabilities.  
  3. Implement Kelly Criterion sizing and automated trade execution using GTC, GTD, FOK, or FAK orders based on projected slippage.20

## **6\. Live Failure Modes & Fallback Behaviours**

To ensure reliable operation under real-time race conditions, the strategy engine must handle data dropouts, network latency, and API failures without crashing or losing state.

### **Live Failure Modes & Fallback Behaviours**

| Failure Mode Event | Primary Source Trigger | Detection Mechanism | System Fallback Behaviour |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Telemetry Ingestion Dropout** | Network interruption or OpenF1 API server outage.5 | No WebSocket telemetry messages received for ![][image148].5 | **Predictive Extrapolator**: The engine estimates current track position using the driver's last known sector pace, updating progress on a simulated baseline lap until the real telemetry feed recovers. |
| **API Rate Limiting (HTTP 429\)** | Exceeding Polymarket's public REST API limits.20 | The server returns HTTP status 429 Too Many Requests.20 | **Exponential Backoff**: Active API calls are paused. The system switches to a 10-second backoff timer, doubling the delay after successive failures, and defaults to caching the last valid order book states in the local database.20 |
| **Model Optimization Staleness** | MINLP non-convergence or solver timeout on the VPS.14 | Continuous optimization execution time exceeds a strict ![][image149] limit. | **RL Policy Fallback**: The continuous non-linear program is terminated, and the system falls back to the pre-trained reinforcement learning policy, which resolves strategy decisions within milliseconds.14 |
| **Unannounced Track Neutralisation** | Delay in official Safety Car messages from Race Control.5 | Telemetry data shows multiple drivers slowing by ![][image150] per lap while the official track status remains GREEN.5 | **Pace-Inferred Override**: The system overrides the official status, switches the local track status to SAFETY\_CAR, and updates pit loss calculations to prevent missed pit stop windows. |
| **WS Disconnect & Out of Sync State** | Network drop causes missing events on the WebSocket stream.21 | Heartbeat signal from Polymarket's WS feed is missed for ![][image151].20 | **Full Re-Sync**: The active WebSocket connection is closed and re-initialized. The system queries REST endpoints to fetch the latest order books and update the local database state.20 |

(PDF) A state-space approach to modeling tire degradation in formula 1 racing, accessed on June 1, 2026, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/404228712\_A\_state-space\_approach\_to\_modeling\_tire\_degradation\_in\_formula\_1\_racing](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/404228712_A_state-space_approach_to_modeling_tire_degradation_in_formula_1_racing)

F1 regulations tweaked after three races with new cars \- Racecar Engineering, accessed on June 1, 2026, [https://www.racecar-engineering.com/articles/f1/f1-regulations-tweaked-after-three-races-of-new-cars/](https://www.racecar-engineering.com/articles/f1/f1-regulations-tweaked-after-three-races-of-new-cars/)

18. A simulation framework for Formula 1 race strategy based on pit-stop optimization, accessed on June 1, 2026, [https://optimization-online.org/2026/02/a-simulation-framework-for-formula-1-race-strategy-based-on-pit-stop-optimization/](https://optimization-online.org/2026/02/a-simulation-framework-for-formula-1-race-strategy-based-on-pit-stop-optimization/)

20. What Is Polymarket's CLOB? Central Limit Order Book \+ How to Get ..., accessed on June 1, 2026, [https://www.quantvps.com/blog/polymarket-clob-central-limit-order-book](https://www.quantvps.com/blog/polymarket-clob-central-limit-order-book)  
21. Open F1 Python API Docs | dltHub, accessed on June 1, 2026, [https://dlthub.com/context/source/formula-one](https://dlthub.com/context/source/formula-one)

[image5]: <data:image/png;base64,>
